sentences.text: My name is Felicia Carmelly.
That's C-A-R-M-E-L-L-Y.
And my maiden name is Steigman.
That's S-T-E-I-G-M-A-N.
I was born in September the 25th, 1931.
I was born in a little town in the province of Bukovina, in Romania.
And the name of the town is Vadra-Dorna, which is V-A-D-R-A, hyphen, capital D-O-R-N-A.
My father's name was, was Isaac Steigman.
They called him [?
Yitzhak ?] at home.
My mother's name was Laura Steigman.
And they called her [?
Lipsha ?] at home.
No, I did not have any.
My father had a store.
And my mother helped in the store sometimes, but most of the time she was busy with being a compulsive housewife.
That means that her floor was shining at 5 o'clock in the morning, more than my kitchen table ever did-- and everything else, of course.
My mother was very bright.
She only had six grades of public school, but she was extremely well-read.
She was very knowledgeable on a variety of subject matters-- very into politics, economics, history.
She was very involved in, in everything that was going on around her.
She was very domineering.
She had to have her way.
When I was a child, I often got beatings from her because I didn't do what she wanted me-- particularly when I didn't eat.
And she considered that it's very important that I be fat because if anything happens, I'll have resources to draw on, if I'm fat, if anything happens.
My father was the gentlest man I ever met, except for my husband.
They're very much alike, interestingly enough.
He was very quiet, very soft-spoken.

It was-- I don't know what the English name for that is, but he was selling fabrics and fur vests for peasants, and fur hats, boots, and needles, and thread, and shoes, shirts, all those kinds of things, which primarily geared for the peasants in the surrounding communities, surrounding the town where we lived.
On the main street, of course.
But I don't know the name of the main street.
I forgot.
I don't-- 10 Minutes walking distance.
The town as a whole was inhabited by about 2,500 Jewish people.
There were some Christians in town who were professionals and merchants, or clerks, administrators, working for government institutions, and so forth.
Most of the Christians would live in the surroundings of the city, in small villages.
And they would come in for shopping or to bring their wares to the market, to the town.
And they would sell the produce from the farms.
The Jewish life in town was very active.
There were two synagogues.
One was called the temple, and that was a very fancy building with light blue ceiling with little lights in it, which looked like stars.
And I loved going there.
And it had columns, and the women were, of course, sitting on the-- on the upper floor, even though this was considered a more liberal orthodox synagogue.
Then there was another one, which was just a plain hall.
And that was considered the orthodox synagogue.
But there were a lot of activities.
There were social circles, and there were book reviews, and there were music recitals.
And Jewish theater used to come from other places, and there were choirs.
And there was always something to do.
And the city was-- the city was in-- situated in the Carpathian Mountains.
So it was a resort town where a lot of people used to come to spend their vacations, wintertime and summertime alike-- more so, summertime, but wintertime too because it was a ski resort.
So in the summer a lot of people would come from Bucharest, which is the capital of Romania, or all kinds of other places where it got much hotter than it was in the mountains.
And we made a lot of friends.
I mean, my parents made a lot of friends with all these people.
And we made a lot of connections, which were helpful to us when we were deported-- through these tourists that used to come, mostly Jewish people, also.
Most of the Jewish people in town were merchants.
They were also clerks, and there was a big wood mill where a lot of people worked.

There was, of course, a shochet and rabbis, and the barber, and the baker, and the tailor, and, and the matchmaker, and the whole structure, the whole richness of, of Jewish life in Romania, which was very rich because Romania was-- had the third-largest Jewish community in Europe, after Poland and Russia.
Or I believe it was Russia and Poland.
I believe in Russia, there were more.
And Jewish life was very vibrant and very energetic.
And there were a lot of Zionist organizations for youth and for adults.
And everybody--
I did.
But before the war, I was only 10 years old.
So I belonged to an organization called Hanoar Hatzioni, which was sort of a middle-- well, we gathered, and we danced, and whatever.
I think--
Oh, at least once a week, at least.
And my mother was involved in Hashomer Hatzair, which was a more leftist Zionist organization, which later fed into her and my inclinations towards communism, before we knew what it was all about.

I always liked to organize things with or for other kids-- be it a funeral for a bird, where the whole city of kids came and we put the bird in a shoebox, or what have you, and there was a whole procession through the streets of the city.
I used to like mountain climbing.
I built a factory of some sort in our wood shed, and I had a lot of kids involved in that.
I was always organizing something.
I was always a very active child.
And it was also an activity which kept me away from my mother, because I was afraid of her.
I was afraid that if I don't finish my food, I'm going to get it.
And I got it, every day.
And often I was black and blue.
I attended public school.
But I had a private teacher for Hebrew and Jewish history.
Yes.
Yes, there was-- the town was too small to have a Jewish school just for Jewish kids.
So most of the Jewish kids had private tutoring after school, on a one-to-one basis, actually.
It was not an organized school.
It was-- there were a few Hebrew teachers and history teachers in town.
And they used to come to the house, and they used to give us lessons.

In school, I encountered-- I-- I became aware of anti-Semitism for the first time when, when my teacher called me out in front of the class and took the, the attendance book-- which was a big book with black covers-- and she hit me with it on the head.
And she said, I'll never grow anymore because I'm a Jew.
And I was-- as it was, I was the smallest in the class.
So I really-- that really--
About nine.
It meant that I'm different, that maybe there's something wrong with me, that I'll never make it, that I'm not the same like the other kids.
Yeah.
Yeah, there were a number of us in-- Jewish kids-- in the same classroom.
I don't remember what their lot was, but we played together with the other kids.
I mean, there was no separation between Jews and non-Jews.
We were good friends.
And we went fishing together, and we crossed the river together to go to school from home-- of course, without our parents knowing.
We had to take our shoes off to get there.
In the winter, we could cross on the ice, but going on the bridge was too conventional.
And that was-- there was a bridge only five minutes away, but we liked to cross the river.
And it was-- I liked school until that particular incident.
And then I sort of felt betrayed by the teacher, whom I liked before.
And, and I felt I don't belong.
I don't belong there because I'm different.
I don't remember if I spoke to my parents.
But I know that for a few days after that, I refused to go to school, and I pretended to be sick.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I just went back.

But that was at the same time when like-- it was after Kristallnacht in '38, 1938, when everybody-- when, when people gathered, while before there were parties, and laughter, and music, and jokes-- people started to whisper to one another.
And we were sort of kept-- the kids were kept-- away from what's happening.
But because the atmosphere changed and because people-- the adults were whispering all the time, I knew that there was something going on.
And I knew that it's not good.
And it became worse and worse.
Then became-- then they started with the laws of wearing the yellow star.
Prior to '38, life was absolutely wonderful.
There was a peaceful harmony to our life.
And there was some kind of a flowing structure in which-- like every day was a day for a specific thing.
Like the girl comes to do the wash on Mondays, and the other one comes to do the ironing on Tuesday.
And the shopping day is on Thursday because we're preparing for Shabbat.
And then cooking is Friday-- because my mother, on Thursday, had to help my father in the store because Thursday was market day.
Every day had a specific function, and it was all good.
And that the smells in the kitchen were just fantastic.
And my mother was a terrific cook, and she loved to bake and to cook.
And she had great parties.
And people would come, and everybody would be delighted, and people would have a wonderful time.
And it was-- it was a beautiful life.
Well, Shabbat started Friday evening when my father came home earlier from his store.
And the table-- my mom and I would set the table, and the candles, and the challah, and everything.
And then, even though we had a tub in the house, and we had running water-- which not many homes in the city did-- and we had cold and hot water in the house.
But on Friday, Friday, late afternoon, there was a tradition that everybody would go to the steam bath, which was not too far from us.
And we called it the shvitz.
And shvitz meaning, perspiring, sweating, and there you go.
The steam bath, you sweat.
And I did not like that at all, but everybody went.
So I went, too.
I didn't like it, number one because I couldn't breathe well because of the steam, and because I had to be in the nude.
And I-- that-- even though we were just the women, separate, of course-- but I still didn't like to expose my body.
So I didn't like it, but I had to go.
And then we came home, and then it was dinner with singing.
And we usually had guests.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my father always went to shul, to the temple.
Sometimes I did, sometimes.
Fri-- Saturday mornings I went more than Friday night, because Friday night I helped my mother clean up.
And there was a routine to everything we did.
There was-- it was almost like there was nothing unexpected in that life.

Everybody knew what tomorrow is going to be like, or what next week is going to be like, or when the tourists are going to come, or when the music is going to start to play in the park in the gazebos for the tourists.
And those Romania-- I don't know if in all of Romania, but where we lived-- the four seasons were absolutely three months, each season.
So come March the 1st, we were actually wearing a little brooch with some red and white ribbons, to signify the, the coming of spring.
And that was when we put away the winter clothes.
And it was spring.
And it really was spring.
So every season had-- and that sort of added to the fact that you knew what to expect, And you knew how it's going to be tomorrow.
And then suddenly, you didn't know.
Well, Pesach was a really big deal, a really big deal because we had an attic in the house.
Our house consisted of four apartments, which my paternal grandparents built because they had three children.
So they built an apartment for each of the children and one for themselves.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It didn't turn out that way, but that's what happened when they built the house.
And so the third floor of the house was an attic where we kept the Pesach dishes, and all kinds of seasonal things.
So getting the dishes down from the attic, and washing them and shining them and this was a big thing.
I mean, it took about a week or two to clean the house from chametz and get all the-- the tablecloth were different for Pesach.
Everything was-- the-- the candlesticks were different for Pesach.
Everything was different.
And the house, of course, was always-- my mother was compulsively clean.
Everything had to shine.
And we had a lot of crystal in the house, and a lot of Chinese porcelains and stuff.
And everything was shining, and everything was sparkling.
And the-- the smells of the food, and the-- it was just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- it's very moving to me, to remember those times because I've never had them again.

On the one hand, I was a very pampered because I was an only child, and because I was the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] grandchild to my grandparents and the first niece to my aunts, who lived in the same town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So on the one hand, I was spoiled.
And on the other hand, I was-- my mother was very, very strict with me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My aunts absolutely adored me.
My, my grandmother had three daughters, and that was-- and they were all fantastic as in they were all helping her, because she was a widow.
Her husband died in the First World War, as one of the very few Jewish officers in the Austro-Hungarian army.
And I believe my mother was five years old when, when he died.
And then the next-- my mother was the oldest.
The next one was my Aunt Sidi, who was two years younger than my mother.
And the youngest was my Aunt Mila, who I don't believe ever saw her father.
Or I-- it's even possible that my grandmother was-- I think I heard some stories of my that she was born after he was killed.
So the last time he was on leave, home, was when my grandmother was pregnant with her.
And being left at that time, with, with three girls, was very difficult for my grandmother because you had to prepare dowry.
And also, she was not coming from a rich family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were no widow's pensions in those times.
The big thing that she got from the government, because she was a war widow, was she was allowed to sell cigarettes and stuff, which was not sold in stores.
So she used to have a table with a stand, with an umbrella, with whatever, in front of the stores, and used to sell.
And that's how she brought up the girls.
But the girls all helped in, in supporting the house in one way or another.
Well, first of all, my father's family was much more well-to-do.
So my grandmother was hoping that if my mother marries somebody who is rich, by those standards, she will help with the dowry for the other two girls, which also didn't turn out that way.
But that was the plan.
And eventually, my, my grandmother also opened up a little store, a few years before the war broke out.
And she was doing a lot better.
My Aunt Mila, the youngest, was always helping her in the store until she got married.
And my Aunt Sidi married [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody who used to come as a tourist to Vadra-Dorna, which is also called Dorna, by the way, for short.
And they lived in Belz, which was in Bessarabia.
And they were very, very well-to-do.
And he was madly in love with her, so he didn't expect any dowry.
And so she moved.
And then my Aunt Mila married.
Her husband's name was Uncle Armin-- I mean was Armin And he was from another part of Romania, from Transylvania.
And my grandmother was extremely concerned that she doesn't know en-- know enough about him.
She doesn't know enough about his family background, and, and what is his financial situation, and all of that.
So and she was against-- she was basically against this marriage.
But he said that he will forego the dowry.
And my grandmother said that she doesn't want him to forego the dowry.
She will give it to him in 10 years' time.
If the marriage goes well and he is a good boy, he'll get the dowry after 10 years.
But of course, after 10 years-- things have changed very much.
She actually offered him a dowry after 10 years, which was after the war.
But he refused it.
And this Uncle Armin, who was really very-- he was brought up-- he studied in the yeshiva.
And he was in the cheder, and he was a terrific merchant.
And he never had a store or anything, but he was always dealing and wheeling with things, and he always had money.
And he was actually instrumental in saving our lives when we ended up in camps.
I don't remember.

I just, I think that I-- I think everybody thought that life was going to continue the way it was, with on Monday we do this, and on Sunday we do this, and on Friday we do that because that's how the whole town lived.
The whole town was in this beautiful, peaceful structure of when we go for walks in the park and when we go to the movies.
And this was all set.
Life started changing in '38.
And it changed when anti-Semitism began to be felt more.
It changed more in, in '39, and even more in '40, when we were supposed to wear the yellow star.
And there were curfews, and--

I was-- aside from a few anti-Semitic incidents like the one that start-- that I experienced in school, it didn't affect me, personally, except for the fact that the adults around me were whispering and keeping things away from me.
And I knew that those were terrible things, otherwise why would they keep them away from me.
So that was-- I started to be afraid of things.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I just--
Sometimes I was afraid to go out in the dark by myself.
Sometimes I was afraid of noises that were not familiar, or I became much more aware of what's happening around me than I was before.
Before, I was sort of in a dream, in a daydream, and everything was nice and beautiful.
And suddenly, I was hit with a reality, which wasn't that nice.
And I was told to keep quiet in school.
And I was told not to complain in school.
Yes.
Well, the students usually take after the teacher.

And if the teacher says that you-- you're not good because you have blue eyes, or because of whatever, then the students will say the same thing, that you're not good because you have brown eyes, or because you're a Jew.
I don't remember that they said anything, but the attitude changed.
Like we didn't laugh together, or play together, or run together that much.
They didn't come to the house anymore.
We didn't meet in the park anymore.
We didn't go mountain climbing together, to the extent to which at the age of nine or 10 we could mountain climb.
But we always-- everybody had a rucksack there, and everybody was going up to the mountains to watch the town in the valley, and to, to gather berries, and to listen to the music in the gazebos.
And all that finished.
Also, there were soldiers that-- there were more soldiers in town, Romanian soldiers.
So going to the park was no longer a pleasure because the people were afraid of them.
Yeah, because the, the Iron Guard and the government-- Antonescu's government in Romania-- was very anti-Semitic.
And the army, of course, was a government institution.
And they were-- I mean, it's not that they weren't anti-Semitic before.
They were, but it was sort of buried.
And on the surface, people were nice to one another, and people exchanged things, and people got together.
